sentences.text: Adrianus Van As, A-D-R-I-A-N-U-S, short Adrian.
Yes.
No.
Adi.
16th of April, 1919.
Amboina.
A-M-B-O-I-N-A.
That's in the Malukus in Indonesia.
My father had the same name as I.
My mother, well, she had a lot of names.
Hendrika Antonia Christina Johanna.
[SIGHS] The eldest one is called after my mother.
Just the same number of names too.
The second one is called after the name of my wife's mother, Nelly Margaretha.
And the last one is called after my father, so after me, too.
Adrianus.
And he shortened it off, now.
[INAUDIBLE] is Adrian.
How many?
Five brothers and five sisters, yeah.
My father was a teacher.
Yeah, well, what about my life in Indonesia?
I think that's what a normal child goes through.
We had a good life, easy life, in that time.
A lot of help around us so we could concentrate more on our hobbies and sport and whatever we wanted to do.
Not much to do in the house, around, because everything was taken up by the servants we had.
So quite a comfortable and easy life, yeah.
Well, after high school, I went over to Holland for to further my studies at the nautical college in Holland.
So I wanted to become a deck officer.
To finish, I studied in Holland, but I couldn't-- have never used it.
Well, Amboina, we lived in Amboina only for two years.
So I know hardly anything about Amboina itself.
We were there in 1991.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So that was first time I saw Amboina myself as an adult.
So now I know where I was born.
Because my father was a public servant, as such, and we moved around over Indonesia all the time.
You stayed only a couple of years here and there.
The last couple of years because we were going to high school, and that's where my father had to move to Batavia, what's now called Jakarta.
Otherwise, we couldn't go to any sort of high school.

Only in the capital cities, there was a possibility of high schools or otherwise you had to be sent over to Holland or to other places in Indonesia, if there was a possibility to go there or to have the place for my father there itself.
Yeah.
Presbyterian.
Yes.
My father was an organist, and I was an organist too later on.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, so far, what I recall now is in 1934, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1935, around about that surrounding, that Jewish shop was opened.
One of the bigger shops, and it was called [INAUDIBLE].
And my mother knew him from her child days.
So that's where we got the invitation to be there by the opening.
You could say that that's the actual contact what we got to know as a Jew, but a Jew didn't mean anything to it at all.
There was no difference.
I mean, religion, we never talked about religion.
Who was at school?
OK, that were your school friends.
But sometimes-- sometimes, not all the time-- that you met someone in church.
But for most of the people, you saw hardly anyone on the Sunday.
Were you aware of what was happening in Europe, the rise of Hitler?
Oh, we got to know some, especially when the socialism came up in Germany.
So that was in the beginning of the '30s.
It bothered us, insofar at school, I mean.
We made jokes about it.
The Germans were calling 'heil Hitler,' which at [INAUDIBLE] we have a different name.
We call it 'hallelujah.' [INAUDIBLE].
We were playing around with it.
I noticed it when I went over to Holland myself in 19-- that was in 1937.
And I boarded a train from Genoa to Amsterdam.
And when we crossed the border into Germany, that's were I saw the actions of the Germans there.
And that was already, well, something I didn't like at all.
You saw on the-- on all the stations which we passed, there were all soldiers.
And we were not used to military at all-- I mean, in Indonesia you could, you saw your soldiers, but only at parades and for the rest, you hardly noticed them, they in the country.
I went over there for my studies.
I went to nautical college in Delfzijl, in the top of Holland, north, just close to the German border.
The opposite side was was Emden.
That was in '38.
Two years.
I completed the study all right.
But too late to go back to Indonesia, because we intended to go back in July, 1940-- my wife and I, at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were cut off in May.
Everything was packed.
We had 11 cases already standing, waiting for us.
But we had to unpack a year thereafter, after our eldest one was born.
Holland is not a country what shows, actually, anti-Semitism.
There's a certain sort of form of anti-Semitism, yes, but it doesn't show up, actually.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You notice it, in the wartime, that it became more noticeable and that we saw it actually existed.
But more or less it-- because of the press influence, it was shaping the difference between the Jews and the rest of the population.
Already, in 1941, they started off as the first measurements against the Jewish people but that they were not allowed in the parks and the hotels and in the trams and trains and so on.
And that's where, actually, already I started to resist a whole lot.
In the beginning, the German occupation was, say, more on a friendly basis.
And they said, oh, well, they are not too bad, although I didn't trust them at all.
They were occupying us, and that was already against me.
Because I was depending on monies, in the first instance, from Indonesia.
And that was cut off already too.
So I had to make a living.
That was 1940.
So it affected me from the beginning on.
Well, firstly, after a couple-- I went, firstly, to Sweden.
Tried to get away.
And I was there in October-- October '40.
And I came back by the end of October to prepare myself for the second time and then move over to England.
I could've been in England within three hours after having arrived, actually, in Sweden.
So that quickly was possible, after having made your connections.
And the Germans were not taking any action at that time either.
If someone went, OK, then nothing was done.
But by the end of 1940, that's where they said, OK, if someone leaves the country, we take measurements against the family.
So I couldn't risk that either.
But by the time I came back, my wife was already expecting and so that makes it impossible.
Bertha from [INAUDIBLE].
Well, then, I had to look for a job.
And I applied by the department-- Overseas Department from Holland-- if there was a possibility of getting some money to survive.
And they allowed me an allowance after two or three months, and in the meantime, I had to look for a job.
And that's where I started off working in the food distribution.
That was practically the only one available, because shortly after the arrival, they already were picking up as much foods as possible out of Holland and transferring it to Germany as a gift.
It was mentioned as a gift from the Dutch population, yeah.
But we haven't given it at all.
They took it.
Well, to further their own wants and needs in Germany.
And we had very good reserves in Holland, very good ones.
But within a year, we notice it, of course.
And that's where the food distribution started.
And gradually everything came into distribution.
So for that, they needed people to employ the offices, and I was one of the first who got a permanent job out of it there.
Yeah.
First it was on temporary basis, only for issuing the-- the coupons.
But later on, it became more a permanent basis.
And, actually, I got a permanent job out of it there.
Eh, yeah, well, because of my resistance already from the beginning, I came automatically, but there was no, actually, underground movement.
There was nothing of the sort.
So everybody who was doing it was doing it on his own and gradually got some friends around you who were helping you out.
And so I came to the conclusion that in November I was actually involved in it all together in a group, yeah.
And that's where the underground work started.
Well, in the first instance, my neighbor was a captain in the Dutch army.
That's where my first connection was built, but from there on, we got gradually other people who became interested and wanted to sabotage.
Well, the first instance was the government in exile dropped leaflets and so on in Holland by the planes who were flying over from Holland to Germany to bomb the other country.
So we collected those leaflets and distributed by those people who we knew were interested in those.
So some more about what was going in England, because in the first instance we expected, oh, well, the war will be over within a year's time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the whole start of-- now, later on, we-- we started to collect certain things the Germans were doing and transmitting what they did to England by radio.
So, shortly thereafter, all the radios were picked up by the Germans and confiscated.
Everybody had to get rid of all the copper, silver, what have you.
Radio, radiograms, all these things.
They were collected.
You had to bring them to a certain spot and they took them.
Gone.
Oranje.
Oranje was the name, yeah.
Oranje.
How do?
How do you contact each other?
Well, you couldn't do it by phone.
So I got used to not to talk in the house even, because the walls had even ears.
That's what we were thinking.
So most of the discussions followed outside.
I did with my wife too later on.
For the family, I didn't want to know it-- even for the family what we were doing.
So if I had something to tell in which she was involved or could be involved, then I told her outside.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You had to be very careful, yeah.
Yeah.
You could say yeah, and you could say no.
There was no exchange of what we were doing.
Certainly not.
But at the end, when I was already appointed the camp commander of Camp Westerbork in the period of [INAUDIBLE] when the Germans were leaving at that time.
There was no connections even at that particular time.
I got to know even in 1949-- that's when I met other people from other groups.
And they said we had the intention of taking over Camp Westerbork, but then we got the message that you were going to take over.
And that's what the first connection, what I actually noticed, that were between our group and another group.
It was called [?
Pereau. ?] That was some sort of connection, but for the rest, no.
We were all in separate groups.
The combination of all these groups were built up after war action, not during the war at all.
Certainly.
I was following that from the beginning on, yeah.
That's where I got my first contacts, actually, with the Jewish people.
Got to know that they were Jewish people, you could better say that.
I knew quite a number of people but not as Jews.
So that's where we got more acquainted.
Yeah.

We were discussing and the friend says when they-- before the time already, there was some connection, but as soon as they were building it up to wear the yellow star, Star of David, and then it became more [INAUDIBLE], because I remember there one Sunday that for the first time that they had to wear those Stars of Davids.
And everybody who would like to show that they were not in agreement what the Germans were doing nipped their hats or greeted them.
That's the first sign where you got to see that the people were not agreeing with what the Germans were doing.
But what could you do more?
I mean, we were in occupation.
Too openly you couldn't show it because then you were picked up.
Oh, that's a story that I forget, actually.
What happened was, when we were at war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the beginning of May in 1940, that actually the occupation started off on the 15th, and the war started on the 10th.
Because I was from Indonesia, and Indonesia was still a free country, and in Indonesia all these [? christlings-- ?] we called them [? christlings-- ?] the people who were in favor of Germany.
And that was the party, the NSB, they have it picked up in Indonesia, because the [INAUDIBLE] they are French or with the German.

So there was a reaction in Holland after that, that for those people who were picked up in Indonesia, the Germans and those NSB-ers, they were looking for people who were related to those families in Indonesia.
And I was one of them, and they came up one day at the front door to pick me up.
But, luckily, I wasn't home at that time.
And so I went under straightaway the day thereafter into the country.
And actually it was then closed by [INAUDIBLE] already at that time.
So I was under for about six weeks.
The Germans.
Germans did.
They were picking me up.
But I was there, so that was a lot.
So I was under for about six weeks.
But they were only once.
I expected them to come back the day thereafter, so I went straightaway on the-- and they never returned.
The ones they could pick up at that time, yes, and they took them as hostage.
And not all of them have been-- been in a camp for a long time, but they were for some time anyhow arrested and to stay there.
But, really, they didn't do very much in that particular time.
So after six weeks, I came back again and into the open, showed myself.
But that was the first reaction already shortly in the war.
In July-- that was in July '40, yeah.
Now my life started, so far as possible.
Yeah.
Well, that was in food distribution is where I started off.

And because of my connections, when they started off by transporting Jewish people out of the country, that's where I was contacted if I could do something about it too or if I was willing to do something about it.
So I went over to Westerbork in July '42 and came in contact with the camp commander-- the death camp commander-- that was [INAUDIBLE].
And they were looking for an employment for me to get me into the camp so I could do what I wanted to and see if he could sabotage everything what they were doing.

And he appointed me to his aid, the camp commander, just as his aid, so that they could get the full picture of what was going on, if I was employed in a, say, a normal position, you had hardly any contact, and you didn't know what was going on there at all.
But because of that appointment, being his aid, I got to know everything.
What was going on.
All the letters which were written by the Germans and what their intentions were.
And I got to know, of course, right away the organization in the camp as well.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans got hold of Camp Westerbork on the 1st of July, 1942.
But they never were there at the particular time.
Actually, they took over the camp, not any earlier than on the 1st of January 1943.

And before that time, there was only someone there as a contact person between the Dutch government and the German government, because the full camps was, by that time, under control of the Dutch government and the Department of Justice.
That was the department.
And I was appointed then by the Department of Justice as well.
But the day thereafter, the contact person got to know that there was a new person in the camp appointed, and he complained about it.
Who did the appointment?
So I couldn't leave the camp.
I was a prisoner in the camp too for about four or five weeks.
Couldn't leave the camp until they found out how my appointment came into being.
So there I was doing nothing and said, yeah, what's going to happen now?
And the underground was-- our group was trying to find out if there was other possibilities to keep me there.
Now, because of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they knew, already, more.
But because of the fact that I couldn't do anything at all and couldn't move in the office with [INAUDIBLE] and had to stay outside, the only thing what they could do was move into the camp.
Because in the camp itself, it was completely free.
And there I got the chance of my life to get to know the whole organization, the people there.
Oh, yes.
But let me finish this first, because otherwise I'm losing my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thread here.
The-- so I was completely idle, couldn't do anything.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's where I'm a bit out.
What was I going to say here?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you stop it here a second?
I was completely idle there now.
Oh, yes.
That was the build up of the underground movement.

They had to try to keep me in some sort of the other way there in the camp itself now, because they knew already that the intentions were to bring quite a number of Jewish people into the camp and transport them from there on to Germany, that there was a need for a food distribution office.
And the coupons, the people who were coming in hardly had any with them than their own luggage, and the coupons weren't there.
So they couldn't buy the stuff needed for their-- their living.
And there, the contact came with another department.
That was the Economic Department, under which the distribution was formed.
And they said, well, the best thing we can do is form an office under direct control of The Hague, where the head office was, and appoint someone there.
And that's where they found me.
He said, well, he has got already the experience in the food distribution.
Why not him?
But that was at the time at the [INAUDIBLE].
What was I, 23?
But then a leader could not be appointed before the age of 25.
So I got one who was appointed over me, and he was a bad fellow.
He was really nasty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But because the fact that I was already there, I had to-- to bring the office into being, order all the stuff what we needed for the office and so on.
So I was the only one, up until the 12th of October.
That's where the office actually opened, and that's where he came and-- Who is he?
That's Moss.
Moss was his name.
And he was nasty, really nasty.
He was nasty with the Jewish people too.
And I was trying to get as many people into the office as possible to keep them back from transport.
He was Dutch.
Oh, yeah.
So I was [INAUDIBLE] from the beginning on with him, and the head of us came to know that and then [INAUDIBLE].
And that was my luck actually.
He thought he could do better than the German contact person, and he thought that he could talk it over with him, and tell him what he wanted to do.
And that's what the German contact person didn't like either.
So he advised The Hague to get rid of him and said, yeah, who can then be appointed, because I was still of the same age?
So there was no other possibility.
Then someone from The Hague came over, and he said, there is only one who can take over here and that was me.
And I got the transfer to the leadership not straightaway but in actual fact, yes, but the appointment didn't follow up until the 1st of March, 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, what want you to know from the organization of the camp?
We'll break for the end of the tape there.
